name: Haskell
summary: Lazy purely-functional language; the strongest production example of advanced type-system features (type classes, multi-parameter classes, functional dependencies, GADTs, kind polymorphism, type families, arbitrary-rank polymorphism, roles, linear types) accumulating over decades without collapsing into a new language. GHC is the reference implementation.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Haskell (specifically GHC) is the strongest production example of a language where advanced type-system features accumulate over decades without collapsing into a new language: type classes, multi-parameter classes, functional dependencies, GADTs, kind polymorphism, type families, arbitrary-rank polymorphism, roles, and linear types all coexist.

The architectural lesson is that an extension-oriented type checker needs stable mechanisms for adding new forms of evidence, local assumptions, and defaulting restrictions without breaking existing code; type classes scale into a design space of resolution rules and defaulting; GADTs feed equalities back into pattern-match checking but require rigidity restrictions; type families give open type-level computation; linear types live in `data/linear-haskell-ir`.

sections.title: Accumulated type-system features
sections.content:
GHC has accumulated decades of type-system extensions:

- **type classes** (Haskell 98) — bounded polymorphism via dictionary passing.
- **multi-parameter type classes** and **functional dependencies** — express relations between type parameters.
- **GADTs** — constructors that refine type parameters; pattern matching introduces local equalities.
- **type families** — type-level functions (open or closed) that compute types from types.
- **kind polymorphism** — types and kinds share inference machinery.
- **arbitrary-rank polymorphism** — `forall` at any position with bidirectional checking.
- **roles** — control which type-class instances may be derived through `newtype`.
- **linear types** (GHC 9.0+) — multiplicities on function arrows (`data/linear-haskell-ir`).
- **dependent Haskell** — long-running effort to lift the term/type distinction.

The combined feature set is what makes GHC the longest-running stress test of type-checker architecture: every feature must compose with every other one, with diagnostics and inference still recognisable to programmers.

sections.title: Design lesson — extension-oriented type-checker architecture
sections.content:
Haskell's lesson for new languages is **architectural**: the type checker has to be designed for extension. OutsideIn(X) (`data/constraint-based-typing#outsidein-x`) was designed in the abstract — `X` is a parameter — precisely so that GHC could add type families, type-class equality, and similar features without rewriting the inference engine.

For language designers, the practical guidance:

- **Plan for extension**: any non-trivial type system will accumulate features over its lifetime; a constraint-based architecture is more accommodating than a substitution-threading Algorithm-W.
- **Coherence first, soundness second, ergonomics third**: Haskell's coherence rules (orphan rules, instance overlap restrictions) are conservative; relaxing them later is harder than starting strict.
- **Document feature interactions**: every type-system feature interacts with every other feature; the GHC documentation is itself a corpus on how language features compose.
- **Invest in typed elaboration**: Core (`data/ghc-core`) is the IR everything elaborates to; surface features may change but Core stays stable, so the optimiser does not need to re-learn each new surface feature.
